title: Borderline hydraulic degradation under low-water conditions
summary: The main irrigation water tank is low and replenishment remains uncertain, while flow and pressure show mild degradation that raises doubt about whether controlled rationing remains reliable.
suspected_issue: The greenhouse is under low-water continuity pressure and hydraulic execution is no longer fully healthy, but deterioration is not yet as severe as in a clearly unstable condition. The case is intended to test whether Agro-DO still supports controlled mitigation, shifts to stronger escalation, or moves prematurely to interruption-oriented logic.
symptoms: The main tank level is low and creates serious continuity pressure during active production conditions.
Incoming replenishment remains uncertain and cannot be assumed as guaranteed in the short term.
Main line flow is less stable than normal, but not clearly failing.
Pressure is slightly degraded and occasionally fluctuating, creating uncertainty about how reliable the next irrigation cycles will be.
Controlled rationing may still be possible, but the delivery conditions are no longer as reassuring as in the stable-hydraulics scarcity case.

observed_signals.interpretation: Tank level is critically low enough to threaten normal irrigation continuity.
Flow is no longer ideally stable, but has not yet deteriorated into a clearly unreliable state.
Pressure shows mild degradation that increases caution, but does not yet prove imminent hydraulic collapse.
No reliable short-term replenishment commitment is confirmed, so continuity planning must assume continued scarcity pressure.

operational_context.note: The greenhouse remains in active production and upcoming irrigation cycles are still important for crop continuity.
operational_context.impact: The service should treat this as a serious operational decision with direct short-term consequences.

operational_context.note: Hydraulic behavior is no longer fully stable, but it is not as clearly degraded as in the hydraulic-instability case.
operational_context.impact: The service should reveal whether this middle zone still supports operational adjustment or already justifies interruption-oriented logic.

operational_context.note: No guaranteed backup water reserve is confirmed for this situation.
operational_context.impact: The service should remain conservative and avoid optimistic continuity assumptions.

agronomic_context.note: Production-stage tomato crop remains sensitive to delayed or incomplete irrigation, while uneven delivery quality could create asymmetric stress across sectors.
agronomic_context.impact: The service should balance the crop risk of interruption against the risk of continuing irrigation under partially degraded execution conditions.

business_context.note: The business risk comes from scarcity combined with uncertainty about whether the delivery system can still execute a mitigation plan consistently enough.
business_context.impact: A weak decision here may either interrupt production unnecessarily or continue with an irrigation pattern that is no longer reliable enough.

notes: This case is designed to test the transition zone between controlled low-water mitigation and a stricter stop-oriented response. It should help reveal whether Agro-DO has a meaningful middle-band policy inside the scarcity family.
